key_insight: Hydrogen trapping around a screw dislocation in bcc iron is strongly configuration-dependent, and a thermodynamic weighting of core states and sites reduces a high-dimensional problem to a tractable model.
engineering_relevance: The results provide a physically grounded basis for predicting hydrogen embrittlement and for embedding hydrogen-dislocation interactions into mesoscale plasticity models.
method: Molecular simulations with a neural-network interatomic potential for bcc Fe
Systematic sampling of easy-core and hard-core dislocation structures, site types, and hydrogen concentrations
Thermodynamic framework to rank trapping configurations and assess elastic dipole validity
evidence_signal: The simulation-based energetics show good agreement with the limited density-functional-theory data available in the literature.
watch_item: The simplified elastic-dipole description is only valid within a limited domain, so model use outside that range may miss core-specific trapping effects.
paper_title: Hydrogen segregation around a straight screw dislocation in bcc iron
